mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 3.0i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X5 3.0i engine typically costs £2,800–£8,500 supply only depending on generation. A used M54B30 engine can be found from approximately £1,164. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 3.0i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation you're asking about. The M54 (E53, 2000–2006) is bulletproof when properly maintained but cooling system failures are common. The N52 (E70, 2006–2013) is one of BMW's most reliable engines. The N55 (F15, 2014–2018) offers strong performance but has oil leak and timing chain concerns. The B58 (G05, 2019+) is the most reliable X5 variant ever built. Across all, oil-change discipline is everything.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 3.0i?

acceptedAnswer.text: M54 cooling system failures and oil leaks (E53), N52 oil leaks and VANOS recall issues (E70), N55 oil leaks and timing chain wear (F15), and B58 PCV valve and minor cooling issues (G05). See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 3.0i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept E53 X5 (values £3,000–£8,000), a £3,000–£4,000 recon is 30–70% of the car's value — borderline. Against a £8,000–£15,000 E70 or F15, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 30–50% — often worth it. Against a £20,000–£40,000 G05, a £4,500–£5,500 recon is 15–25% — worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 3.0i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E53 (2000–2006) = M54B30; E70 (2006–2013) = N52B30; F15 (2014–2018) = N55B30A; G05 (2019–present) = B58B30 (badged as xDrive40i in later models). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M54 cooling system, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: M54 cooling system failures are always repairable without replacing the engine. Water pump, thermostat, and radiator replacement costs £400–£900. However, if a cooling system failure leads to severe overheating, it can cause head gasket damage or warped cylinder heads — at which point a full engine replacement may be required. At the first sign of overheating or coolant loss, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The X5 3.0i THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: The original X5 3.0i — bulletproof M54 straight-six but cooling system failures, oil leaks and VANOS issues are the key watchpoints
One of BMW's most reliable engines — lightweight magnesium block; oil leaks (valve cover, OFH, oil pan), VANOS recall and cooling system are the main concerns
BMW's first turbocharged 3.0i — strong performance but oil leaks, timing chain wear and cooling system failures are well-documented
The most reliable X5 variant ever built — B58 is BMW's modern masterpiece; PCV valve and cooling system are the only watchpoints

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current X5 3.0i range. Older generations: see the Era Map above for E53/M54, E70/N52, F15/N55 and G05/B58-specific pricing. A used M54B30 engine can be found from approximately £1,164, while reconditioned six-cylinder engines typically command higher prices.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X5 3.0i engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The X5's engine bay is reasonably accessible, but the SUV height adds some complexity. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (M54 — E53)
problems.symptoms: Overheating, temperature gauge creeping into the red zone, coolant loss, steam from engine bay, sweet smell, low coolant warning.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see the temperature gauge rising, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: The M54's cooling system is its most significant weakness. Water pump failures, thermostat issues, and radiator leaks are common. The water pump impeller can fail without warning, leading to sudden overheating. If you notice the temperature gauge creeping up or any coolant loss, have the system inspected immediately. Proactive replacement of the water pump, thermostat and hoses at 80,000-mile intervals is recommended preventative maintenance.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (M54 & N52 — E53, E70)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage around the valve cover, oil filter housing or oil pan, low oil level warning.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are among the most common issues on both the M54 and N52 engines. The M54 is prone to leaks from the valve cover gasket, oil filter housing gasket, and rear main seal. The N52 shares these issues, with the valve cover gasket, oil filter housing gasket, and oil pan gasket being common failure points. The "Mickey Mouse flange" coolant connector is also a known leak point on the N52. If you notice oil spots under the car or a burning smell, have it inspected promptly.

problems.title: VANOS System Failure (M54 & N52 — E53, E70)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise at startup, loss of power, poor low-end torque, reduced fuel efficiency, rough idle, hesitation on acceleration.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect variable valve timing

problems.recommendation: The VANOS system, which controls variable valve timing, can be a source of headaches on both the M54 and N52. Common issues include rattling noises at startup and loss of power and efficiency. The M54's VANOS seals can degrade over time, causing a loss of low-end torque. The N52 had a 2023 recall affecting 155,627 vehicles, where bolts securing the VANOS mechanism could loosen or break. If you hear a rattling sound at startup or notice a loss of power, have the VANOS system inspected.

problems.title: N55 Oil Leaks & Timing Chain Wear (F15)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, rattling noise on cold start, loss of power, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The N55 engine in the F15 X5 suffers from oil leaks around seals, gaskets and the cylinder head, as well as timing chain wear (after 100,000 km). Some owners have reported increased oil consumption at higher mileages. A cold-start rattle is the early warning sign for timing chain issues — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes with the correct BMW-approved oil are the best prevention.

problems.title: B58 PCV Valve & Cooling System (G05)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, oil in the intake, white smoke from exhaust, check engine light, low coolant warning.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can cause drivability issues

problems.recommendation: The B58 engine is widely regarded as BMW's most reliable modern straight-six, but it has minor known issues. The PCV valve can fail, causing rough idle and misfires. Valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket leaks can also occur. Cooling system issues are generally less severe than on earlier engines. Regular maintenance and early detection will keep the B58 running smoothly for hundreds of thousands of miles.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW X5 3.0i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it before overheating causes engine damage
Seal or solenoid replacement restores performance
Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement
Minor repairs; the B58 is exceptionally reliable
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 3.0i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals
* E53 (2000–2006): cooling system overhauled (water pump, thermostat, radiator)
* E70 (2006–2013): VANOS recall completed (2023 recall) and no oil leaks
* F15 (2014–2018): no cold-start rattle and oil leaks addressed
* G05 (2019+): B58 engine — the most reliable choice
* No overheating history on any generation
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — these engines are durable but unforgiving of neglect
* Any overheating history — potential head gasket or warping damage
* E53/E70 with significant oil leaks — gasket replacement is labour-intensive
* F15 with cold-start rattle — timing chain work is imminent and expensive
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive faults

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW X5 3.0i has evolved from a naturally aspirated workhorse into a turbocharged masterpiece over four generations. The E53's M54 is bulletproof but demands cooling system vigilance. The E70's N52 is one of BMW's most reliable engines. The F15's N55 offers strong performance but has timing chain and oil leak concerns. The G05's B58 is the most reliable X5 variant ever built. Across all generations, one rule applies: oil-change discipline is everything. BMW's extended service intervals are widely considered inadequate — treat your X5 3.0i to 8,000–10,000 mile oil changes and it will reward you with hundreds of thousands of miles of driving pleasure.

cta.label: → Compare X5 3.0i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 3.0i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattles (M54 VANOS rattle, N55 timing chain warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M54/N52/N55/B58) and confirmed oil-change discipline (8k–10k miles max)
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates potential cooling system issues
* Temperature Gauge — Any fluctuations? Cooling system may be failing (M54/N52)
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve cover, oil filter housing and oil pan
* VANOS Operation — Any hesitation or loss of low-end torque? (M54/N52)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Cooling, VANOS, timing, or PCV codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to fuel system or PCV issues)
